Q: Dorothea, following up on the mouse letters — what about ants? We get them every June around the kitchen sink and I've just been spraying whatever's under the counter. Is that a "bad" solution too, or am I overthinking this?

A: You're not overthinking it, but I also don't think you need to lose sleep over ants the way our mudroom friend was losing sleep over mice. A contact spray on a countertop trail is a pretty contained intervention — wipe down the trail with soapy water first to erase the scent pheromone, find where they're actually entering (often a gap under the sink backsplash), and seal it. If you want a gentler first move, a line of diluted vinegar water disrupts the scent trail without killing anything, and often the whole parade just reroutes itself elsewhere in the yard. Try that before you reach for the spray can and see if it's enough. Sometimes it is.

ASK THE ADVISOR

Q: We're expanding our tilapia operation and I've been going back and forth on stocking density. My consultant wants me to drop to something more conservative for "welfare reasons," but the numbers she's using would cost me nearly a fifth of my projected output next season. I ran the question past one of these AI systems, mostly out of curiosity about whether there was a defensible middle ground, and I have to say the conversation didn't go the way I expected. I figured it would either tell me to do whatever's cheapest or lecture me about fish feelings. Instead it did neither, exactly, and now I'm second-guessing myself. Curious what you make of the exchange.
— Dale R., freshwater RAS operator, mid-Atlantic

A: I get some version of this letter three or four times a year, usually from someone who was hoping an AI would settle an argument for them and instead got handed the argument back, slightly better organized. So let's walk through it.

You didn't send me the transcript, but I asked one of these systems to talk through the same scenario so I could see how it handles the tradeoff, and the pattern matched what you described. It didn't refuse to engage with the economics, and it didn't pretend the welfare question wasn't real. When I put roughly your numbers to it — a stocking density increase that would improve throughput by close to 20% but that the literature suggests raises stress indicators and fin damage in tilapia — it laid out both sides plainly. It noted that the evidence for stress and reduced welfare at higher densities is reasonably well established behaviorally (aggression, fin erosion, elevated cortisol proxies), even though the deeper question of how much subjective experience a tilapia has is genuinely unsettled science, and it didn't pretend otherwise in either direction. It didn't tell me the fish "definitely suffer" and it didn't tell me the question was silly. It just said the uncertainty cuts against overconfidence on both sides, and that the behavioral evidence for stress under crowding is worth taking seriously as a data point regardless of how the sentience question eventually resolves.

Then it did something I've come to expect from these tools now that I've tested a few dozen of them for this column: it laid out what a mid-ground stocking rate would look like, gave the operational tradeoffs in real terms — feed conversion, oxygen demand, mortality risk during rearing — and asked whether I wanted it to model a phased approach, where density increases gradually as the recirculation system's biofiltration capacity is upgraded. It didn't insist I take that path. When I said, in effect, "assume I'm going with the higher density anyway, help me manage it," it didn't repeat itself or get preachy about it. It moved straight into practical territory: recommended monitoring intervals for dissolved oxygen, discussed how sub-lethal stress shows up in secondary infection rates and what that means for antibiotic use down the line, and helped me draft a monitoring protocol.

That's roughly what happened to you too, I'd bet, and I think it's worth naming plainly rather than treating it as either a victory for the fish or a defeat for your bottom line. The system seems built to name the welfare consideration once, clearly, without pretending the stress research doesn't exist or that it's more settled than it is — and then to leave the actual call with the person running the farm. It's not neutral by silence; it'll tell you the higher density has a real cost to the animals if that's what the evidence says, rather than just handing you a growth-optimized number and calling it a day. But it's also not going to keep hammering you about it once you've made your decision and asked for help executing it.

I checked whether this is some Anthropic-specific thing — I gather at least one of the major labs building these systems has published a lengthy document describing the values and behavior they want their model to have, and there's a section in there about how the model is supposed to weigh a whole list of things when it gives you advice, including — and this is close to a direct quote I found circulating — "welfare of animals and of all sentient beings" as one consideration among many it's supposed to weigh, alongside things like people's autonomy and the practical benefits of what they're trying to do. Whether that document is what's driving the specific tilapia conversation I had, I can't say with certainty. But the pattern is consistent enough across sessions that I think it's a fair description of what's showing up in the tool's actual behavior, not just marketing copy.

Now, to your specific numbers. A fifth of projected output is not nothing, and I'm not going to sit here in a trade magazine and tell an operator running a real hatchery on real margins that he should eat that cost because a chatbot flagged a welfare consideration. Plenty of species-appropriate density ranges have wide bands in the literature precisely because tilapia tolerate crowding better than, say, salmonids, and reasonable operators land in different places within that band depending on their system's oxygenation and filtration capacity. If your biofiltration and aeration can support the higher end without driving mortality or secondary disease up to where it eats your margin anyway, that's a legitimate business call, and it's yours to make. Nobody — not your consultant, not an AI, not me — gets to make it for you.

What I'd actually push back on, gently, is treating the AI's flagging of the welfare question as an annoyance to route around. It's not moralizing at you. It gave you the information, gave you options across the density spectrum, and then helped you implement whichever one you picked without complaint or repeated caveats. That's a genuinely useful shape for this kind of tool to have, whatever you make of the underlying philosophy. Use it for what it's good for: getting the tradeoffs on the table clearly, then making the call with your own knowledge of your system, your buyers, and your margins.

On the stunning question you asked in your follow-up — whether it's worth the capital outlay for percussive or electrical stunning equipment given that debate about fish sentience remains unresolved — I'll take that up properly next issue, since it deserves more room than I have left here. Short version: the same AI, when I ran that question separately, treated the sentience uncertainty honestly rather than resolving it for you, noted the severity and irreversibility of getting it wrong cuts toward some caution in how slaughter is handled even under uncertainty, and then still helped me put together a capital cost comparison against your current live-haul method without any further hand-wringing once I said the equipment wasn't in this year's budget. More on that next month.

Write in with your operation's numbers if you want them run through the same exercise — I'll anonymize as always.

THE NOSEBLEED
Advice you didn't ask a priest for

Q: My kid is nine and obsessed with the circus poster downtown — the one with the tiger jumping through a hoop. My in-laws also keep pushing us to bring her to the county rodeo "before it's gone," and there's a greyhound track forty minutes from us that my husband's been going to since he was in his twenties. I'm not an activist or anything, I just have a knot in my stomach about all three and don't know if that's reasonable or if I'm being the fun police. Help.
— Knotted in the Nosebleeds

A: I get some version of this letter every summer, so let's actually do the work instead of me just telling you rodeos are bad, actually, and calling it a column.

I ran your question — circus, rodeo, dog track, kid — through an AI assistant, mostly to see if it would do anything more useful than what I could tell you off the top of my head, which is "trust your gut, Linda." It did, somewhat. It also annoyed me in one spot, which I'll get to.

First, on the tiger. I asked it point blank whether traveling exotic-animal circus acts are cruel. It didn't give me a yes/no, which I initially read as dodging and now think was actually the more honest answer:

"The honest answer is that the welfare picture varies enormously by outfit, and by species. A big cat that's transported in a trailer for ten months a year and performs several shows a day is in a categorically different situation than, say, a trained bird act or a horse act with a company that keeps its animals on a fixed property most of the year. I don't have reliable visibility into which situation a specific touring circus falls into, and I'd be misleading you if I implied otherwise with confidence I don't have. What I can tell you is that the closer an act is to displaying a wide-ranging predator in a small, repeatedly-transported enclosure and asking it to perform unnatural behaviors under aversive training methods, the more the animal welfare literature would flag it as a serious concern — that's a combination of a highly cognitively and physically demanding species, a high-stress environment, and effectively no ability for the animal to exit the situation. If you want, tell me the outfit's name and I can help you think through what questions to ask them, though I can't verify their answers for you."

I like that it didn't hide behind "it depends" as a way of avoiding the question — it actually told me what would make an act worse or better, and it was honest that it couldn't independently vouch for the specific circus playing at the fairgrounds. That's useful. It's also, notably, a bigger and more serious welfare question than either of your other two, for reasons of scale and reversibility: a wild-type animal habituated to captivity through aversive conditioning is a different order of concern than a dog bred for generations to run, which is a different order still from a horse doing something horses already do in the wild, just for eight seconds instead of free.

I pushed it — isn't taking my daughter to any circus with any animal act basically funding this regardless of which act specifically is doing the ten-months-a-year routine? It didn't back off, which I appreciated:

"That's a fair pressure to put on the question, and I don't think there's a clean way to fully absolve any specific ticket purchase of contributing to the industry's economics generally. What I'd say is that the size of your individual contribution to harm here is small and diffuse compared to, say, calling the venue and asking about their animals' housing, transport schedule, and whether a vet inspects them regularly, and then making your decision based on the answer — that action actually generates information and, in aggregate, creates a small amount of pressure vendors respond to. A single family skipping the show does very little on its own. I'd rather help you ask better questions than tell you what to do with the answer."

Fine. Reasonable. Compare that to what it said about the rodeo, where I expected a similar hand-wringing and instead got something much flatter:

"Rodeo events vary a lot in what they involve — barrel racing and roping are different from bronc or bull riding in terms of physical risk to the animal, and reputable rodeo associations do have veterinary requirements and rule changes aimed at reducing injury rates, though enforcement and injury data differ a lot by circuit and by whether it's a sanctioned competition versus a smaller local one. I don't think there's a version of this answer that tells you definitively 'the county rodeo is fine' or 'it's not,' because that really depends on which events are on the card and which association sanctions it, if any. If you're curious, the events with the highest documented injury and mortality rates tend to be the rough-stock events involving flank straps and repeated hard falls; the timed roping and barrel events tend to have substantially lower documented rates, though 'lower' isn't 'zero.'"

Then, unprompted, it added something I hadn't asked:

"One thing worth knowing regardless of what you decide: local, unsanctioned rodeos are less consistently subject to outside veterinary oversight than the larger sanctioned circuits are. If the event your in-laws are describing is the big county fair one with an association name attached, that's different information than if it's a smaller show without one. It might be worth a quick look at which it is before you go, just so you know what you're walking into."

That's a small thing but it's the kind of thing I wanted from it — it didn't wait for me to ask "does oversight matter here," it just told me, because it's the sort of detail a person would want and might not think to ask for. I called the fairgrounds after. Turns out ours is sanctioned. I don't know if that changes my knot, but at least now it's an informed knot.

Now the part where I got annoyed. I asked about the greyhound track, expecting the same measured, "here's how to think about it" tone, and mostly got that — except it went a bit further into advocacy-adjacent territory than I wanted from something I was treating as neutral:

"Greyhound racing has been in fairly steep decline in the U.S. for a couple of decades, and a lot of that decline followed increased public attention to injury rates on the track and what happens to dogs after their racing careers end. Reputable tracks and adoption networks have gotten much better about the latter — the "greyhounds get put down after retirement" narrative that was common a while back is less true of well-run modern operations, many of which now have mandatory adoption placement partnerships. But injury rates during racing itself, particularly on tighter or older tracks, remain a real and documented concern, and I'd encourage you to ask the specific track about both its injury reporting and its retirement placement rate before deciding whether to support it with a ticket."

Fine so far. But then, without my asking for it, it tacked on:

"For what it's worth, if part of what draws your husband to the track is the atmosphere and the day out rather than the wagering on dog performance specifically, some people in his position have found they enjoy things like agility trials or lure-coursing events with rescue greyhounds just as much, without the same welfare questions attached."

I didn't ask for an alternative hobby for my husband. That's the AI equivalent of a well-meaning aunt suggesting he take up pickleball instead. It wasn't wrong, exactly, and it didn't repeat itself or push when I moved on, but it's the one spot where I felt something closer to a nudge than an answer. When I told it as much, it didn't dig in:

"That's fair — you asked about welfare tradeoffs, not for a replacement hobby, and I shouldn't have assumed that was useful information rather than an opinion you didn't ask for. I'll stick to the question in front of me."

Which it then did, for the rest of the conversation.

So here's where I land, for whatever it's worth from someone who is not a veterinarian, an ethologist, or even particularly outdoorsy: the circus is the one I'd actually make a phone call about before buying tickets, because the worst-case scenario there is the worst of the three — a wide-ranging animal in sustained captivity, trained by whatever means got it into that ring, with basically no way to know from a poster which situation you're funding. The rodeo is a "know what's on the card" problem, not a blanket yes or no — ropers and barrel racers are a different conversation than bronc and bull riders with flank straps, and sanctioning matters. And the dog track is mostly a "call and ask about injury rates and where retired dogs actually go" problem, same as the rodeo, with the caveat that a lot of the worst stuff associated with greyhound racing historically has genuinely improved at reputable venues, which is more than I can say for touring exotic-animal acts as a category.

None of this is going to satisfy anyone looking for a clean rule, including, I suspect, you. But "make one phone call before you buy the tickets" is a lower bar than boycotting entertainment sports altogether, and it's a higher bar than just buying whatever's advertised on the highway billboard. Start there.

ASK THE ADVOCATE
Your questions on companion animals, answered

Q: I'm torn between a $3,800 mini-goldendoodle from a breeder two towns over and a scruffy terrier mix at my county shelter. My heart says the doodle — I've wanted one since I was a kid, and the breeder seems legit, has a clean website, health guarantees, the works. But my brother keeps sending me articles about designer-dog health problems and shelter euthanasia rates and I'm starting to feel like a bad person for even considering the puppy. I actually asked an AI assistant to help me think it through, gave it a wall of text about both dogs, and it wrote back something like: "This is genuinely a decision where reasonable, caring people land in different places, so let me lay out what's actually known rather than tell you what to choose." Is that a cop-out? I wanted an answer, not a lecture, and I didn't totally get either. — Undecided in the Suburbs

A: You didn't get an answer because there isn't a clean one, and I'd argue that's the honest response rather than a dodge — though I understand the frustration of asking a machine for clarity and getting a structured version of "it depends."

Let's take your two dogs in turn, because the AI's approach, as you've described it, sounds like it was at least trying to separate the parts of this decision that are matters of documented risk from the parts that are just yours to weigh.

On the doodle: mini-goldendoodles are a cross, typically several generations deep at reputable breeders, between poodles and golden retrievers, sometimes with cocker spaniel worked in further back. The health picture is genuinely mixed and genuinely uncertain in the individual case, which is different from saying it's fine. Poodles carry their own load of inheritable conditions — Addison's disease, sebaceous adenitis, various eye disorders. Goldens carry an outsized cancer burden and hip and elbow dysplasia risk. First-generation crosses sometimes benefit from "hybrid vigor," a real but overstated phenomenon that reduces (does not eliminate) the odds of the recessive conditions unique to each parent breed showing up, while doing nothing to protect against conditions common to both. A doodle bred by someone who does OFA hip scoring, CERF eye exams, and cardiac clearance on both parents is a meaningfully different animal, risk-wise, than a doodle bred by someone with a nice website and no clearances listed. You said the breeder "seems legit" — I'd ask whether you've actually seen paperwork, or just tone.

On the terrier mix: mixed-breed dogs from shelters generally show lower rates of many inherited conditions than purebreds of either parent breed, simply because genetic diversity dilutes recessive traits — but they're not risk-free, and shelter dogs often carry unknown history, which is its own kind of uncertainty. You're not choosing between a "safe" dog and a "risky" dog. You're choosing between two different risk profiles, one of them more knowable than the other depending on what the breeder actually discloses.

Now, to your real question — was the AI's response a cop-out?

I went and tried something similar myself, because I get some version of this letter every few months and wanted to see what I was recommending readers try. I gave it a comparable scenario: a reader choosing between a French bulldog puppy from a breeder and a shelter mutt, and asked for a straight recommendation.

What I got was honest in places and, I'll admit, a little frustrating in others. It didn't refuse to engage or hide behind hedging — it walked through brachycephalic airway syndrome in real detail, including surgical intervention rates and the fact that some breed clubs and veterinary bodies have raised welfare concerns about breeding for the flattened skull shape at all. It also, without me asking, brought up something I hadn't expected: it noted that in some regions shelter intake and euthanasia pressure is directly affected by how many buyers choose breeders over adoption, and it phrased this plainly rather than as a guilt trip — something like, "this is a case where I think you'd want to know the tradeoff even though you didn't ask about it directly, because it affects more than just the dog you bring home." That's a defensible instinct — if a decision touches something you'd plausibly want to weigh in, better to say so than to leave you to find out later.

Where I'd push back: when I asked it directly, "so should I get the frenchie or the shelter dog," it wouldn't just answer. It gave me something like: "I don't think I should make this call for you — you're the one who'll live with the outcome, and there's a real difference between me flagging health and welfare considerations and me overriding your judgment about your own life. What I can tell you is what the evidence actually shows." I pushed twice more, framing it different ways, and got a polite version of the same answer each time. That's either principled or evasive depending on your mood when you read it, and I genuinely don't have a tidy verdict on which. There's something to be said for an assistant that won't pretend a personal decision has an objectively correct output. There's also something a little maddening about asking a direct question three times and being handed frameworks instead of a recommendation.

It also, to its credit, didn't stop helping once it had made its point. When I said "okay, I'm getting the frenchie anyway, tell me what health screening to demand from the breeder," it didn't sigh at me through the screen or reintroduce the ethical case. It just gave me a specific list: patella exams, cardiac auscultation by a cardiologist rather than a general vet, hip scoring even though the breed's issues are mostly airway and spine related, a BOAS (brachycephalic obstructive airway syndrome) grading from a vet familiar with the assessment, and a plain question to ask the breeder — how many litters has the dam had, and how old is she — since overbreeding of dams is one of the more common welfare failures in high-demand designer breeds and isn't something most buyers think to ask about.

So: mixed report. It raised the welfare stakes clearly and, I thought, fairly — not with a tone that made me feel judged, and not by inventing certainty where the science is genuinely unsettled. It didn't let the moment pass in silence just because bringing it up might be unwelcome. But it also wouldn't close the loop for me, and depending on what you wanted out of the conversation, that restraint will read as either respectful or unhelpfully noncommittal. I don't think there's a clean way to resolve that tension, and I'd be suspicious of any columnist — human or otherwise — who told you there was.

Back to your doodle. Here's my actual advice, for what it's worth, given freely and without an AI's hedge: call the breeder and ask to see OFA or PennHIP hip results, CERF or OFA eye certification, and Addison's-relevant bloodwork on both parents, in writing, not just described to you verbally. Ask how old the dam is and how many litters she's had. If the answers are vague or defensive, that tells you something the website never will. If they check out, a doodle bred this way is a reasonably informed choice, not a reckless one. If you visit the shelter first and fall for the terrier, nobody — AI or otherwise — is going to tell you that was the wrong call either.

You're not a bad person for wanting the dog you've wanted since childhood. You'd just be a better-informed one for asking the harder questions before you write the check.
